Program Overview:
The Solano County Black Infant Health Program (BIH) works with participants to develop life skills, reduce stress, build social support, and improve overall health and wellness. BIH provides education on the importance of early and continuous prenatal care, well-child checkups, breastfeeding, and timely and complete immunizations to ensure babies are born healthy and grow into healthy children.
Black/African American babies are more than twice as likely as White babies to die before their first birthdays.
Black/African American birthing people are 3 – 4 times more likely to die from pregnancy related complications than their White counterparts.
The mission of the Black Infant Health (BIH) Program is to help more babies make it to their first birthday - and beyond - by ensuring their mothers have healthy pregnancies.
We aim to reduce the risk of Child Welfare Service (CWS) interventions by elevating the nurturing relationship and providing opportunities to learn about and implement positive parent and child interactions.
Our program is designed to support women to thrive during one of the most vulnerable, and beautiful times of their lives: their birthing and perinatal journey.
Our program honors the unique history and traditions of Black/African American people by presenting information in a culturally centric and affirming manner.
Who is Eligible?
Solano County residents
Self-identified Black/African American birthing person who:
- is pregnant
- or has at least one child who is less than 2 months old
Self-Identified Black/African American caretakers of children 0-17.
Services Offered:
Healthy Families Solano (HFS):
Light Touch Case Management:
Mocha Mommas Thrive (MMT):
Solano HEALs (Health Equity for African American/Black Lives in Solano):
